https://salorak.es/.body:
Welcome 🌊
Hello there and welcome to my humble personal website.  You will be wondering, what am I going to find in here?. Well then, you are probably going to find blog posts about anything I've been working on, I've worked in the past or I'm thinking about it, but mainly technical.  Also, you'll find the projects I've developed or contributed to in the Open Source community.
Finally, you will get to know a little bit about me.
But where can I find all of those things?
First of all, if you want to search by content you can use the icon  on the top left of the navigation bar.
Also you can use the handy shortcut Control + k to open the search popup directly!
And of course, you can use the following links:

    
    
          /home  : Homepage, exactly where you are.
    
    
    
          /blog  : Posts like a blog. Thoughts, works and working articles.
    
    
    
          /projects  : Projects I've developed or contributed to. Sometimes, projects I'd love to contribute to.
    
    
    
          /about  : If you want to know more about me, who I am and what I do, this is the place.
    
    


Contact
Hey! If you want to contact me you can do so by sending me an email at salorack@protonmail.com
https://salorak.es/.title: Homepage

https://salorak.es/about/.body:
Hi there! I'm Hector Alarcon, a software security engineer focused on building internal tooling, research and development as well as systems engineering. During my 4+ years of experience as an engineer I fell in love with the concept of systems. What do I mean by systems?
From Oxford Dictionary a system is:

A set of things working together as parts of a mechanism or an interconnecting network; a complex whole.


    
    


To me, understanding how computers work is essentially understanding what parts take place, why are they necessary, what is their sole definition and how they interconnect with all the other parts. This is why I love Linux. Linux gives you the freedom to explore each module at your own pace.


    
    
    
        In the internet I go by the name of SalOrak. And the logo representing me the most is the one on the right side.
As a computer engineer I explored many territories, some wilder than other. What I discovered during this adventure is that I love to grease the wheels, to find annoying connections and smoothen them. The good thing is I have been in many perspectives along the way: as a Cybersecurity Analyst, Software Security Researcher, Fullstack Web-developer and even Teacher!. But above all, I'm an explorer. And that is something I hope is visible in my GitHub Profile

    
      


Hey! If you want to contact me you can do so by sending me an email at salorack@protonmail.com
https://salorak.es/about/.title: About me

https://salorak.es/blog/hello-world/.body:
Hello!
Hello there! If you are reading this its because the website and the blog section is opened.
I've been a passionate developer for a couple years now, and I have some opinions and thoughts about how the technology works.
I've worked in many workplaces, probably too many, but hey! I've discovered a ton of ways to work, to make things work, to build, to manage, to communicate and to team up.
I do am specially excited for this section, as it is the foundation for my voice in the internet.
With everything, thank you for passing by and I hope to see you around!
https://salorak.es/blog/hello-world/.title: Hello World!

https://salorak.es/projects/cursed-matri.body:
Cursed Matrix
A simple matrix effect written in C using the ncurses library.
I've been using many guides, packages, plugins, applications, utilities and public forks over the years. This project is a naive attempt to give something out there. It is simple, basic but it kinda does the job.
https://salorak.es/projects/cursed-matri.description: A simple matrix effect written in C using the ncurses library, thus the name. It is not finished but it works relatively well on Linux machines.
https://salorak.es/projects/cursed-matri.title: Cursed Matrix

https://salorak.es/projects/dotfiles/.body:
Dotfiles
This project takes  HEAVY inspiration on tsoding bash script to configure his dotfiles. The script is quite easy as it only loops over a MANIFEST, a file containing a table with the path to dotfiles and the destination path, and configures everything.
The main differences with tsoding script are the following:

I pretty much overengineer'd the MANIFEST to make it look more human readable.
Added a backup support, where it allows to use the my configuration files and revert the changes at any time. Do not do it with directories!
Added arguments to allow versatility.

Oh! And it contains my current configuration files. Take a look at it, you will be surprised I joined both worlds: Emacs and Nvim.
https://salorak.es/projects/dotfiles/.description: A simple bash script to configure (and link) all my configurations files. It also contains my current configuration files.
https://salorak.es/projects/dotfiles/.title: Dotfiles

https://salorak.es/projects/qmk-corne/.body:
QMK Firmware - Corne
The qmk_firmware github repository is a fork of the official QMK Github. I've build (and soldered) a split keyboard to use as my daily driver. This split keyboard is the famous Corne Keyboard from Foostan which has a total of 42 keys, 6 of which are thumb keys.
In this repository I also have my custom keymaps based on Dvorak for Alpha keys. Check it out!
One thing to mention is that I added navigation keys to move my mouse using the keyboard.
https://salorak.es/projects/qmk-corne/.description: A QMK fork where I keep my Corne's various layouts. Mainly Dvorak and Qwerty ones.
https://salorak.es/projects/qmk-corne/.title: QMK Firmware

https://salorak.es/projects/whaler-el/.body:
Whaler.el
Whaler.el is a port of the whaler.nvim neovim plugin to the Emacs ecosystem.
It is based around the same concept but adding a more customizable API for Emacs users to work with.
Whaler offers a tailored experience to managing your projects. It is a minimalistic and highly customizable project manager.
Whaler acts as a completing-read function extension to move blazingly fast between directories as well as have a minimalistic sense of working directory whilst providing an easy to use API to work with directories.
Whaler.el is a package to move between directories while maintaining a sense of working directory, i.e a project manager.
Don't forget to check it out, it is available on MELPA.
https://salorak.es/projects/whaler-el/.description: A port of Whaler.nvim to the Emacs ecosystem. It is a minimalistic and highly customizable project manager. Available on MELPA.
https://salorak.es/projects/whaler-el/.title: Whaler Emacs

https://salorak.es/projects/whaler-nvim/.body:
Whaler.nvim
Whaler.nvim is a neovim plugin that uses the incredible UI and functionality of Telescope to allow fast-switching between directories.
On setup you add which directories contain the projects you want to keep an eye on and that's it!
The plugin is made with customization in mind, so don't forget to check that out too!
In case you are looking for the repository, click here to go directly ;)
https://salorak.es/projects/whaler-nvim/.description: A Neovim telescope extension plugin to move between projects blazingly fast, using the same concepts of the tmux-sesionizer from ThePrimeagen.
https://salorak.es/projects/whaler-nvim/.title: Whaler Neovim
